research_approach: Web searches targeting FamilySearch, Ancestry, WikiTree, USGenWeb, RootsWeb, PeopleLegacy, FindAGrave, GenealogyTrails; primary source documents where accessible. Four key gaps addressed: (1) Dovie's mother identity, (2) William Leander Byrd's parents, (3) colonial Virginia Byrd ancestry, (4) Westmoreland County VA records.

gaps_addressed.gap: Dovie's mother: Martha Alice Bedford vs. Lillie Byrd conflict
gaps_addressed.status: STILL UNRESOLVED — no new primary source found
gaps_addressed.new_findings: No web-accessible record (census image, death certificate, obituary) was located in this research pass that names Dovie's mother. Milam County Historical Commission information index (milamcountyhistoricalcommission.org) does not list a Bedford or Byrd family inquiry connected to John A Byrd's wife. The mother-name conflict (Martha Alice Bedford per KcaMarco GEDCOM vs. Lillie Byrd per burial co-location with Otha Lee Byrd Sr. in Clute TX) remains exactly as documented in prior passes. The 1910 Milam County Census for John A Byrd's household (FamilySearch collection 1727033) and John A Byrd's 1928 death certificate from the Milam County Courthouse remain the definitive unaccessed resolution records.

gaps_addressed.gap: William Leander Byrd's parents — who were they?
gaps_addressed.status: PARTIAL ADVANCE — BIRD surname confirmed in 1850 Franklin County AL census index; intermediate generations identified from cousin GEDCOM tree are partially corroborated by independent sources
new_findings: The 1850 Franklin County Alabama census index (USGenWeb Archives, S-K Publications) contains entries for the surname BIRD at pages 197B and 198A. Note: the spelling is BIRD not BYRD in this index. William Leander Byrd born 1832 Frankfort, Franklin County AL would be approximately 18 years old in 1850. A BIRD entry at pages 197B-198A is consistent with a young William Leander appearing in a parental household under the BIRD spelling variant. The actual image of these census pages was not accessible in this research pass (USGenWeb server returned connection refused).

No BYRD-spelled entry was noted in the Franklin County AL 1850 census index excerpt — the index uses BIRD, not BYRD, suggesting the family may have used both spellings interchangeably in different records.

The 1860 Franklin County Alabama mortality schedule (1860 deaths only) contains NO Byrd or Bird entries — consistent with William Leander being alive in 1860 and not yet having a close family member die in that calendar year.

A sibling of John A Byrd (1868-1928) was newly identified from Ancestry records in this pass: Leander Byrd (born ~1868 Birmingham, Jefferson County AL, died April 24, 1894, Prentiss County, Mississippi), also a son of William Leander Byrd and Margarete Rhetta Perideaux. The shared migration from Alabama to Mississippi-area is consistent with post-Civil War patterns for this family.

The cousin GEDCOM chain proposes: Benjamin Franklin Byrd (1798) → John Howard Byrd → Michael Byrd (b. 1784 Botetourt VA, d. 1857 Marshall MS) as the path to William Leander Byrd. However, this path has a critical internal problem: Michael Byrd (1784-1857) died in Marshall, Mississippi, and his three known children (via Patsy Logan) are not yet identified by name in accessible open-web sources. The chain through Michael Byrd to Franklin County Alabama is not corroborated by any independent source found in this pass.

Alternative hypothesis: William Leander Byrd's father may NOT be the Mississippi Michael Byrd (1784-1857) but a different Byrd individual settled in Alabama. The BIRD entries at pages 197B-198A of the 1850 Franklin County AL census are the most promising avenue for resolution.
gaps_addressed.confidence_change: No change to UNKNOWN for William Leander Byrd's specific parents. The BIRD census index entry is suggestive but the image was not retrieved.

gaps_addressed.gap: Colonial Virginia ancestry — John Henry Bird/Byrd ~1700 Westmoreland VA and earlier
gaps_addressed.status: SIGNIFICANT ADVANCE — the genealogical chain from Richard Bird (1750-1803) backward through Richard Byrd (1717-1808) to John Byrd (1725-1775) is now better documented, with WikiTree primary-source citations; a plausible connection from this documented line to William Leander Byrd (1832 AL) requires three intermediate GEDCOM-only generations
new_findings: Richard Bird (1750-1803): WikiTree Bird-3176 documents him as born November 3, 1750 in Albemarle, Westmoreland, Virginia. His father is listed as John Henry Byrd (1725-1775) of Spotsylvania, Virginia. He married Elizabeth Woods (Buster) Byrd in 1774 in Virginia. He moved to Fincastle/Botetourt County VA then Hawkins County Tennessee where he died March 5, 1803. Primary sources cited include: Albemarle Parish Register, Surry and Sussex Counties VA (births 1717-1778); Spotsylvania County records (1763 deed); Lord Dunmore's War military records; Historical Register of Virginians in the Revolution (Gwathmey 1987); Montgomery County militia lists (1777-1790); Hawkins County Deed Book I and Will Book (1803); DAR Ancestor database A133396. This is the strongest primary-source documented ancestor in the extended chain.

Richard Byrd will (Hawkins County TN, March 5, 1803): Primary source — the actual will text is accessible via TNGenWeb (tngenweb.org/hawkins/byrd-richard-will/). It names his children as: William Byrd (eldest son), John Byrd (second son), David Byrd (third son), Michael Byrd (younger son), Richard Byrd (younger son), James Byrd (youngest), Charles Byrd (youngest), Jean McDaniel (daughter), Ann ___day (daughter), Elizabeth Byrd (daughter), Mary Byrd (daughter). Spouse Elizabeth Byrd received use of all property during widowhood.

Michael Byrd (1784-1857): Confirmed by Ancestry.com as son of Richard Byrd (1750-1803) and Elizabeth Woods-Buster. Born Botetourt, Virginia. Married Patsy Logan. Died November 2, 1857 in Marshall, Mississippi. Only 3 children documented — their names and Alabama connections are not confirmed by open-web sources in this pass.

Rev William Byrd (1774-1856): Find A Grave memorial 134708782 documents him as son of Rev Richard Byrd and Elizabeth Woods Buster, married Lydia Adair in 1794. His children include: Sarah (married John Wilson), Isaiah (married Asanah Walls), Elizabeth Caroline (married Jonathan Burleson), John (married Sara Fears), Susannah E (married James DeMent), Richard C. This William + Lydia Adair marriage matches the GEDCOM chain exactly (GEDCOM records 'Rev. William M. Byrd + Lydia Adair'). The son John Byrd who 'married Sara Fears' maps to the GEDCOM's 'William John Byrd (1799-1855) + Sarah A Fears' — the names are slightly different (John vs. William John) but the Fears/Fear surname match is striking.

John Henry Byrd (1725-1775), father of Richard Bird (1750-1803): WikiTree Byrd-390 and Byrd-379 note that this person's parentage is explicitly undocumented. Multiple genealogies incorrectly list him as son of William Byrd and Bridgetta Huling of Birdsboro, Pennsylvania — WikiTree labels this 'undocumented and unlikely.' His mother is given as Sarah (Jones) but maiden name unconfirmed. His father is unknown in any primary source. He died circa 1775 in Prince Edward County, Virginia.

Richard Byrd (1717-1808): Appears in FamilySearch (LKXX-X3L) and familypursuit.com as father of Richard Bird (1750-1803). Born 1717, died 1808. Spouse Sarah Jones (1729-1810). However, WikiTree and genealogical discussions note researcher confusion between this Richard (1717-1808) and John Byrd (1725-1775) — both are documented as father of Rev. Richard Bird (1750-1803) in different trees. The FamilySearch entry LKXX-X3L lists Richard Byrd (1717-1808) + Sarah Jones as parents of Richard Bird (1750-1803). WikiTree Bird-3176 names John Henry Byrd (1725-1775) + Sarah Carter Jones as parents of the same person. This is an unresolved parentage conflict at generation 10.

CRITICAL DISCREPANCY FROM GEDCOM: The GEDCOM chain lists 'John Henry Bird/Byrd fl. ~1700 Westmoreland VA' as the earliest ancestor. Independent research identifies the actual documented colonial Virginia ancestor as either Richard Byrd (1717-1808) OR John Henry Byrd (1725-1775) — both about 25 years later than the GEDCOM's ~1700 date. The '~1700 Westmoreland VA' figure in the cousin GEDCOM may be conflating this person with an earlier generation (possibly an unknown father born ~1690-1700). No primary source names a Byrd ancestor born ~1700 in Westmoreland County in Hunter's probable line.
gaps_addressed.confidence_change: Richard Bird (1750-1803) upgrades from POSSIBLE to PROBABLE (multiple primary sources cited). Rev William Byrd (1774-1856) + Lydia Adair identified as a new probably-documented generation. The chain from Michael Byrd (1784-1857) to William Leander Byrd (1832 AL) remains unconfirmed.

gaps_addressed.gap: Westmoreland County VA records and connection to famous Byrd families
gaps_addressed.status: NO NEW CONNECTION FOUND — multiple independent sources confirm Hunter's probable line is distinct from Westover Byrds; earlier ancestor in line is documented in Spotsylvania/Prince Edward/Botetourt Virginia corridor, NOT Westmoreland
new_findings: WikiTree Byrd-379 (John Byrd 1725-1775) explicitly places him in Spotsylvania County (before 1763) and Prince Edward County (1763-1775), NOT Westmoreland County. The GEDCOM's claim that the colonial ancestor was in 'Westmoreland VA' is contradicted by WikiTree's independent documentation placing this generation in Spotsylvania/Prince Edward — different Virginia counties.

WikiTree Bird-3176 (Richard Bird 1750-1803) is born in 'Albemarle, Westmoreland, Virginia' per that record — note: 'Albemarle' here likely refers to the Albemarle Parish within Surry/Sussex County, not Albemarle County. This is consistent with him having Virginia Tidewater origins but in a different geographic cluster than the Westover (Charles City County) Byrds.

The Westover Plantation Byrd line (William Byrd I, 1652-1704) is documented in Encyclopedia Virginia as originating from Shadwell, Middlesex, England, with ancestry in Brexton/Broxton, Cheshire. The Spotsylvania/Prince Edward Virginia Byrd line has no documented English origin identified in any source found.
No primary source establishes any connection between Hunter's probable Byrd line and the famous Westover, Shenandoah (Byrd senators/admiral), or any other notable Byrd family.
gaps_addressed.confidence_change: none — already assessed as NO CONNECTION; this pass further confirms that assessment and corrects the GEDCOM's Westmoreland VA attribution

His son Michael Byrd (1784-1857) is also confirmed via Ancestry records as dying in Marshall, Mississippi. The chain from his descendants to William Leander Byrd (1832 Franklin County AL) runs through one or more unconfirmed intermediate generations.

new_ancestors_confirmed.note: The child 'John Byrd (married Sara Fears)' strongly maps to the GEDCOM's 'William John Byrd (1799-1855) + Sarah A Fears' — the Fears/Fear surname match is highly unlikely to be coincidental. This suggests the GEDCOM's 'William John Byrd' is actually this 'John Byrd' (son of Rev William + Lydia Adair), possibly with a name variation.

Tennessee location (Knox County) is consistent with the family migration from Hawkins County TN (Rev Richard Byrd 1750-1803) to Franklin County AL (William Leander Byrd 1832).

new_ancestors_probable.name: Richard Byrd (1717-1808) or John Henry Byrd (1725-1775)
new_ancestors_probable.relationship_to_hunter: One of these two is PROBABLY the father of Richard Bird (1750-1803); the exact identity is disputed between sources
new_ancestors_probable.confidence: PROBABLE as a real person; UNCERTAIN which individual is the actual parent of Richard Bird (1750-1803) in Hunter's line
new_ancestors_probable.conflict_detail: FamilySearch (LKXX-X3L) lists Richard Byrd (1717-1808) + Sarah Jones (1729-1810) as parents of Richard Bird (1750-1803). WikiTree Bird-3176 lists John Henry Byrd (1725-1775) + Sarah (Carter) Jones as parents of the same Richard Bird. Both cannot be correct. WikiTree Byrd-390 (John Henry Byrd 1725-1775) explicitly warns it is a 'conflation of several profiles not supported by documentation.' The safer interpretation is that Richard Byrd (1717-1808) of Westmoreland VA is the more reliably documented figure, and 'John Henry Byrd 1725-1775' may represent a conflation or naming error.
new_ancestors_probable.source: FamilySearch LKXX-X3L; WikiTree Bird-3176; WikiTree Byrd-390; familypursuit.com/genealogy/byrd_richard/richard-byrd-b.1717-d.1808-1

conflicts_resolved.conflict: GEDCOM places colonial Byrd ancestor in 'Westmoreland County VA ~1700' — this is likely imprecise
conflicts_resolved.resolution: Independent sources place the documented colonial Virginia ancestor(s) in Spotsylvania/Prince Edward County (John Henry Byrd 1725-1775 per WikiTree) or Albemarle Parish, Surry/Sussex County (Richard Bird 1750-1803 per WikiTree primary sources), NOT Westmoreland County. The ~1700 date is approximately 25-50 years too early for the documented colonial generation in this line. The GEDCOM's Westmoreland attribution likely represents a naming/location error or conflation with another Byrd family.

conflicts_resolved.conflict: GEDCOM chain lists 'John Henry Bird/Byrd fl. 1700' as if a separate person from Richard Byrd 1717 and John Henry Byrd 1725
conflicts_resolved.resolution: These are the same generation. The documented colonial Virginia ancestor is either Richard Byrd (1717-1808) or John Henry Byrd (1725-1775) — not a separate 1700-era person. The '~1700' in the GEDCOM likely represents the unknown father of this 1717/1725 generation.

colonial_chain_assessment.note_on_gedcom_vs_independent: The cousin GEDCOM chain uses 'Benjamin Franklin Byrd (1798)' and 'John Howard Byrd' as intermediate generations. Independent research found no corroboration for these specific names. The more likely path is: Richard Bird 1750 (confirmed) → Rev William Byrd 1774 (Find A Grave) → John Byrd/William John Byrd ~1799 (Find A Grave child; GEDCOM) → William Leander Byrd 1832 (Ancestry + GEDCOM). The 'Benjamin Franklin Byrd' and 'John Howard Byrd' names in the GEDCOM may reflect a different Byrd branch (the Rev John Howard Byrd 1820-1912 found on Geni is from Jackson County Alabama, not Franklin County).
colonial_chain_assessment.gedcom_michael_byrd_problem: The GEDCOM chain places Michael Byrd as an ancestor of William Leander Byrd (1832 Franklin County AL). Richard Byrd's 1803 will does name a son Michael Byrd, and Ancestry confirms this Michael Byrd died in Marshall, Mississippi in 1857. However, Mississippi is not Alabama. If Michael Byrd died in Marshall MS in 1857, his children would more likely be in Mississippi, not Franklin County Alabama. This is a geographic inconsistency that weakens the GEDCOM's proposed path through Michael Byrd. The Rev William Byrd (1774-1856) → John Byrd (Sara Fears) path is geographically more consistent (Tennessee → Alabama migration corridor).

huguenot_ancestry_note.status: PROBABLE French Huguenot origin — now supported by Geneanet surname database which indexes the Peradeau surname; Huguenot Society of South Carolina and related databases document Huguenot families in the Carolinas-to-Alabama corridor
huguenot_ancestry_note.significance: If Margarete Rhetta Peradeau (1829-1925, probable burial Sharp Cemetery Milam County TX) is correctly identified as William Leander Byrd's wife, Hunter has French Huguenot ancestry on his Byrd matrilineal line — a distinct European origin traceable to French Protestant refugees who fled France after the 1685 Revocation of the Edict of Nantes and settled in South Carolina, Georgia, and later Alabama.
sources: Huguenot Society of South Carolina ancestor list (huguenotsociety.org)
Geni.com Huguenots of the Carolinas project
huguenot_ancestry_note.confidence_change: Unchanged PROBABLE — still requires the Sharp Cemetery 'Margaret Byrd (1829-1925)' to be confirmed as Margarete Rhetta Peradeau via headstone image or Milam County death record 1925

priority_next_steps.note: NOTE: Y-DNA passes through the paternal line only. Hunter's Y-DNA is Spence (his paternal surname), not Byrd. To test the Byrd Y-DNA, a male-line descendant of John A Byrd (e.g., a son of Otha Lee Byrd Sr.'s sons) would need to be tested.

summary: This research pass significantly advanced the Byrd colonial Virginia chain. The strongest new finding is the independent documentation of Richard Bird (Byrd, 1750-1803) as a real historical figure with multiple primary sources: his parents include a Byrd of Spotsylvania/Prince Edward County Virginia (born 1717 or 1725 depending on source); he served at the Battle of Point Pleasant in 1774 (documented in DAR database A133396); his will survives in Hawkins County Tennessee naming 11 children including Michael Byrd; and WikiTree Bird-3176 cites the Albemarle Parish Register (1717-1778), Spotsylvania County deed records (1763), and Montgomery County VA militia rolls (1777-1790) as primary sources. This makes Richard Bird (1750-1803) the earliest ancestor in Hunter's probable Byrd line with genuine primary-source support. A plausible chain runs from him to William Leander Byrd (1832 Franklin County AL) through Rev William Byrd (1774-1856, married Lydia Adair — matches GEDCOM exactly; documented in Find A Grave) and a son John Byrd who married Sara Fears (also documented in Find A Grave, matching the GEDCOM's 'William John Byrd + Sarah A Fears'). The GEDCOM's proposed path through 'Benjamin Franklin Byrd 1798' and 'Michael Byrd' has a geographic problem: the confirmed Michael Byrd (1784-1857) died in Marshall, Mississippi — not Alabama — making him an unlikely father for William Leander Byrd of Franklin County, Alabama. The more plausible path bypasses the Mississippi Michael Byrd branch. The four major gaps remain: (1) Dovie's parentage (still POSSIBLE only, 1910 census and death cert needed); (2) William Leander Byrd's specific parents (BIRD entries exist in 1850 Franklin County AL census index but image not retrieved); (3) who fathered Richard Bird 1750 (Richard Byrd 1717 or John Henry Byrd 1725 — conflicting databases, both pointing to the same general Spotsylvania VA Byrd cluster); and (4) Dovie's mother (Martha Alice Bedford vs. Lillie Byrd — unresolved). The Peradeau French Huguenot ancestry is further supported by the Geneanet surname database. No connection to the famous Westover Byrd (William Byrd I/II) or Shenandoah Valley Byrd (Senator Harry Byrd, Admiral Richard Byrd) families has been found or is likely.
